summary: Stellar second set with epic He's Gone jam and soaring Moon sequence
blurb: The show shines in its second set, delivering a monster He's Gone jam, a 15‑minute Standing On The Moon/ Miracle stretch and a thunderous Samson & Delilah. Phil's bass leads the band while Jerry dishes out soaring solos; the audience tape captures the energy with surprisingly clear sound.
review: September 16, 1990 at Madison Square Garden finds the Dead firing on all cylinders, especially after the intermission. The first set is solid but unremarkable, building into a second‑set marathon that features a 24‑minute He's Gone excursion, a post‑drums odyssey into Standing On The Moon → I Need A Miracle → Morning Dew, and a blistering Samson & Delilah that bookends the night. Phil Lesh’s booming bass is the backbone of the performance, often described as “all over it,” while Jerry Garcia soars with magical lead lines despite occasional vocal mix dips. Bruce Hornsby’s piano adds a warm 70‑s flavor, and Vince Welnick’s textures punctuate the jams. The drummers deliver one of the era’s most exciting “sheeeaaaat” post‑drums sections, driving the band into uncharted territory. Overall the concert is hailed as a high‑point of the ’90 tour, with the audience tape capturing the raw excitement and an excellent soundboard version offering pristine fidelity for audiophiles.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Most recordings (5 of 10) are marked positive, repeatedly praising He's Gone, Standing On The Moon, Samson & Delilah and Phil's bass work. Mixed reviews note only occasional mix issues, not performance flaws. Two negative entries lack substantive criticism of the music. The consensus is a strongly positive performance, warranting a +1.6 sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture, crystal‑clear fidelity and matches/exceeds Dick’s Picks 9 quality, preserving every nuance of the band’s peak playing.

key_highlights: Second‑set marathon with a 24‑minute He's Gone jam and post‑drums odyssey
Epic Standing On The Moon → I Need A Miracle → Morning Dew sequence
Phil Lesh’s dominant, sparkling bass throughout the night
One of the era’s most thrilling drumming “sheeeaaaat” sections

band_performance.Jerry: Soars with magical lead guitar solos, especially in He’s Gone and the post‑drums jam; vocals sometimes sit low in the mix but his playing remains a highlight.
band_performance.Phil: The night’s star – booming, inventive bass that drives Samson & Delilah, Iko Iko and the massive He's Gone jam; his use of an Aural Exciter adds sparkle throughout.
band_performance.Bob: Provides solid rhythm and vocal work; occasionally buried in the mix but leads the final jam section with confidence.
band_performance.Brent: Bruce Hornsby’s piano adds a warm 70‑s flavor, while Vince Welnick supplies texture and colorful solos that lift the jams despite occasional mix level quirks.
band_performance.Mickey: Delivers a classic arena‑acid‑test drumming feel; his post‑drums interludes help shape the second‑set’s dynamic flow.
band_performance.Billy: Partners with Mickey for powerful, expansive drum passages, contributing to one of the most memorable “sheeeaaaat” moments of the ’90s.
